title: Behavioural economics
summary:
## The big idea Everything so far has assumed a tidy creature economists call *homo economicus*: perfectly informed, endlessly calculating, coolly maximising utility. Real humans buy gym memberships in January and stop going in February; they'll drive across town to save \$5 on a \$15 book but not on a \$500 laptop. **Behavioural economics** studies how people *actually* choose — and shows the departures from rationality are not random noise but **systematic and predictable**. That matters twice over: it explains stubborn market failures (why information campaigns alone don't stop smoking), and it hands governments a whole new policy toolkit — the **nudge**. ## Two kinds of 'bounded' - **Bounded rationality** — people have limited information, limited time and limited brainpower. Rather than compute the perfect choice, they **satisfice**: pick the first option that's *good enough*. Nobody compares every phone tariff on the market; we grab a plausible one and move on. - **Bounded self-control** — even knowing the best choice, **willpower runs out**. Smokers who want to quit, savers who never start, dieters at midnight: the gap between intention and action is a decision failure that information cannot fix, because information isn't the missing ingredient. ## Heuristics and the biases they cause To cope with complexity, people lean on **heuristics** — mental shortcuts. Useful, fast… and the source of predictable **biases**: - **Anchoring** — judgements cling to the first number seen. A '~~\$200~~ now \$120' price tag makes \$120 feel cheap; the \$200 anchor did the work. - **Availability bias** — probability is judged by how easily examples come to mind. Plane crashes dominate the news, so people fear flying and drive instead — statistically the riskier choice. - **Framing** — the same fact lands differently depending on presentation: '90% fat-free' sells; '10% fat' doesn't. 'Survival rate 90%' reassures; 'mortality rate 10%' alarms. - **Loss aversion** — losing \$50 hurts roughly twice as much as winning \$50 pleases (the heart of **prospect theory**). It's why people hold falling shares ('I'll sell when it recovers') and why 'don't lose your discount' beats 'earn a bonus'. - **Herding** — copying the crowd: stock-market bubbles, panic buying, restaurant queues. - **Present bias (hyperbolic discounting)** — the present is overweighted against the future: \$100 today beats \$110 next week, yet \$110 in 53 weeks beats \$100 in 52. Explains under-saving, procrastination and addiction. ## Choice architecture and nudges If choices are always presented *somehow*, then the presentation — the **choice architecture** — is itself a policy lever. A **nudge** alters that architecture to steer behaviour **without banning options or significantly changing prices**: - **Defaults** — the heavyweight champion of nudges. Make pension saving **opt-out** instead of opt-in and participation leaps (UK auto-enrolment took workplace-pension coverage from under half to nearly nine in ten). Same for organ-donation registers. - **Framing and salience** — traffic-light nutrition labels; printing 'most people in your area pay their tax on time' on reminder letters (HMRC's trials measurably sped up payment). - **Positioning** — fruit at the checkout, stairs more obvious than the lift. - **Mandated choice** — forcing an active decision (tick donor: yes/no) without dictating it. **The case for nudges:** almost free, preserve freedom of choice, politically easy, and defaults in particular are astonishingly effective — they work *with* inertia instead of against it. **The case against:** effects can be **small or short-lived** (a cafeteria rearrangement fades; a sugar tax endures); they can feel **manipulative** — 'libertarian paternalism' still steers people covertly, raising ethical questions about who designs the architecture; and a nudge rarely fixes a *structural* problem (no default enrols you into affordable housing). Serious harms — addiction, drink-driving — usually still need taxes and regulation. ## Why it matters Behavioural economics is A2's licence to criticise every model that begins 'assume consumers are rational'. Deployed well, it upgrades your evaluation everywhere: demand theory (Unit 7's marginal utility), merit and demerit goods, even policy design (why an information campaign flopped where a default succeeded). And it is modern, real-world economics — several governments now run 'behavioural insights' units testing nudges with randomised trials.
## ⚡ Quick revision - *Homo economicus* (rational maximiser) vs real people: **bounded rationality** (→ **satisficing**) and **bounded self-control** (weak willpower). - Heuristics → predictable biases: **anchoring, availability, framing, loss aversion, herding, present bias**. - **Prospect theory**: losses loom ~2× larger than gains. - **Choice architecture** = how options are presented; a **nudge** changes it without banning or (much) pricing. - Star nudge: **defaults** (pension auto-enrolment). Others: framing, salience, positioning, social norms, mandated choice. - Evaluate: cheap + freedom-preserving + defaults powerful **vs** small/fading effects, paternalism/manipulation worries, can't fix structural problems. - Use it to critique the **rational-agent assumption** across the whole course.

keyTerms.definition: The study of how psychological factors cause real decisions to depart from the rational model.
Limited information and mental capacity that prevent full optimisation, leading people to satisfice.
Limited willpower that stops people acting on what they know to be the best choice.
A mental shortcut or rule of thumb used to make decisions quickly.
Relying too heavily on an initial reference value when making decisions.
How the presentation of a choice affects the decision made.
The tendency to feel losses more strongly than equivalent gains.
The design of how choices are presented to decision-makers.
A change in choice architecture that steers behaviour while preserving freedom of choice.
The option that applies if a person makes no active choice (e.g. auto-enrolment).

diagrams: No formal diagram is required. You can link behavioural insights to the **merit/demerit good** and **externality** diagrams to explain why consumption differs from the social optimum.
tips: Contrast **‘homo economicus’ (rational)** with real, boundedly-rational behaviour — a strong opening for evaluation.
Have **2–3 biases ready** (anchoring, framing, loss aversion, present bias) with a concrete example each.
Explain **defaults/auto-enrolment** as the most powerful nudge, and cite behaviour like under-saving.
Evaluate nudges: cheap and choice-preserving, but note **paternalism** concerns and possibly small/short-lived effects.
Use behavioural economics to **critique the rational-agent assumption** across micro topics for high-level marks.

revisionNotes:
# Behavioural economics — quick revision

**The break with rationality**
- Standard theory: *homo economicus* — full information, stable preferences, maximises utility.
- Reality: **bounded rationality** (limited info/time/processing → **satisficing**) and **bounded self-control** (know the right choice, fail to act).

**Biases (know 3–4 with examples)**
- **Anchoring** — first number sticks ('was \$200, now \$120').
- **Availability** — vivid events feel likely (fear of flying).
- **Framing** — '90% fat-free' ≠ '10% fat' in the mind.
- **Loss aversion** — losses ≈ 2× gains (prospect theory); holding losing shares.
- **Herding** — bubbles, panic buying.
- **Present bias / hyperbolic discounting** — under-saving, addiction, procrastination.

**Nudges & choice architecture**
- Choice architecture = design of how options appear; nudge = steer without banning/major price change.
- **Defaults are king**: pension auto-enrolment (UK: coverage <50% → ~90%), organ-donation opt-out.
- Also: social norms ('9/10 pay on time'), salience/positioning, mandated choice.

**Evaluation**
- + Cheap, choice-preserving, evidence-based (RCT-tested), defaults very powerful.
- − Effects often small/temporary; 'libertarian paternalism' = covert steering (ethics); doesn't fix structural causes; severe harms still need tax/regulation.

**Use in essays** — the standard critique of every 'rational consumer' assumption (utility theory, merit goods, information campaigns).
questions:
# Practice questions — Behavioural economics **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 1 style — multiple choice **1.** A consumer facing hundreds of broadband tariffs signs up for the first one that seems acceptable rather than comparing them all. This behaviour is best described as: - A — utility maximisation - B — satisficing due to bounded rationality - C — loss aversion - D — adverse selection
▸ Show answer

B. Limited time and processing power make full comparison impossible, so the consumer settles for 'good enough' — satisficing, the signature of bounded rationality.

**2.** An investor refuses to sell shares that have fallen 40%, saying 'I'll sell once I break even', while readily selling shares that have gained. This illustrates: - A — herding - B — anchoring on the purchase price and loss aversion - C — present bias - D — rational expectations
▸ Show answer

B. The purchase price anchors the judgement, and realising a loss hurts more than an equivalent gain pleases — so losses are held and gains are banked (the 'disposition effect').

**3.** Which of the following is an example of a nudge? - A — banning the sale of cigarettes to under-18s - B — imposing a tax on sugary drinks - C — automatically enrolling employees in a pension scheme with the right to opt out - D — subsidising gym memberships
▸ Show answer

C. A default changes the choice architecture while leaving all options open and prices unchanged. A is regulation; B and D change prices — traditional instruments, not nudges.

**4.** A supermarket labels a yoghurt '90% fat-free' rather than 'contains 10% fat'. It is exploiting: - A — the framing effect - B — the free-rider problem - C — moral hazard - D — bounded self-control
▸ Show answer

A. Identical information, different presentation, different response — framing.

## Paper 4 style — data response > A government makes workplace pensions opt-out instead of opt-in for all employees. Within three years, participation rises from 47% to 88%, with the biggest gains among young and low-paid workers. Almost nobody who is enrolled chooses to leave. Critics note that average contribution rates are set low, and that many workers say they 'never really decided' to join. A rival proposal had been a tax relief worth 25% of contributions. **(a)** Define 'choice architecture'. **[2]** **(b)** Explain why traditional economic theory struggles to explain the low participation rate before the reform. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse why changing the default raised participation so dramatically when an information campaign had previously failed. **[6]** **(d)** Assess whether nudges like auto-enrolment are better policy tools than financial incentives such as tax relief. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) The way choices are structured and presented to decision-makers (defaults, order, framing). [1+1]

(b) A rational maximiser with full information would already save the optimal amount — free money (employer contributions) left unclaimed contradicts rationality. Present bias and bounded self-control (intending to join 'next year') plus inertia explain it. [4]

(c) The information campaign targeted knowledge, but knowledge wasn't the constraint — inertia and procrastination were. Defaults harness inertia: doing nothing now produces the good outcome; present-biased people never get around to opting out either. Hence huge, durable effects concentrated on those most prone to inertia. [6]

(d) Nudge: near-zero cost, 41-point jump, preserves choice; but contributions anchored low and 'never really decided' raises autonomy concerns. Tax relief: rewards active savers, scales with contributions — but costly, regressive (helps taxpayers most), and evidence suggests weak take-up among exactly the groups auto-enrolment reached. Judgement: combine — default for participation, incentives to raise contribution *levels*. [8]

## Paper 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain what is meant by bounded rationality and bounded self-control, and how heuristics can lead to biased decisions. **[8]** **(b)** Assess the extent to which behavioural economics undermines the traditional theory of consumer choice. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Limits on information/processing → satisficing; limits on willpower → intention–action gaps; heuristics (anchoring, availability, framing) as shortcuts that misfire systematically.

(b) Undermines: systematic (not random) deviations from utility maximisation; preferences unstable and frame-dependent. Defends: traditional theory still predicts well in aggregate and for routine purchases; behavioural effects are refinements, incorporated (e.g. prospect theory) rather than revolutionary. Judgement: modifies rather than demolishes — but evaluation answers must use it.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain, with examples, how governments can use choice architecture to influence consumer behaviour. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether nudges should replace taxation and regulation as the main tools for reducing consumption of demerit goods. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Defaults (pensions, organ donation), social-norm messaging (tax letters), salience/positioning (labelling, layout), mandated choice — with a real example each.

(b) Nudges: cheap, liberty-preserving, politically easy — but effects modest against addiction (present bias + bounded self-control are strong); no revenue. Taxes: proven price mechanism + revenue; regulation: certainty for severe harms. Ethics cuts both ways (covert steering vs coercion). Judgement: complements, not replacements — nudges augment; they rarely substitute for price and law where harm is large.
